speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Try to avoid--

speaker: interviewee
text: I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Stay away from that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So you will--

speaker: interviewer
text: But I will--

speaker: interviewee
text: --cut it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cut it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I know you're doing fine. You're Doing terrifically, believe me. The, the details are happening, and they're coming in the order.

speaker: interviewee
text: The funny thing, I never hear the others because we are all assigned to a different group. So I hear only myself to talk. I don't know what the others are saying. Probably everybody has a different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I think when we spoke on the phone before, we had a similar conversation. The music and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We ran out on the part just, when they were taken to the shower. That's where we ran out. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Start it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel three. Interview with Judith Rubinstein. You were telling me-- in the middle of telling me about your coming into Auschwitz and the situation. So just continue with that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we get off from the train?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, after-- after the selection process.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, within a few minutes, we were lined up again in fives. So it's order, had to be order. And we were led into the bath house, where there were shower stalls. And they were looking-- asking for, for hairdressers. Anyone can handle to cut the hair machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a few girls volunteered, and they did a terrible job. First of all, we had to strip completely. Nothing. Nothing was-- except our shoes, was allowed to hold on to. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're standing there naked. They came with these machine and they-- and then-- and they shaved my hair completely. Bald, the private parts, everything. We were born, born, newborn. And into the bath-- into the shower, for just a minute. No soap, just a quick, quick cold shower. And then the dresses were ready for us, the gray, long uniform, no underwear, nothing. There was nothing to cover our head. And put on the shoes without stocking, and marched into the camp. So as we were marching between two lines, it was-- we saw children. That-- I find out later, they called it [GERMAN]. That was the gypsies, for a while, until they were all killed. And there was a family [? lager ?] from [NON-ENGLISH]. They brought the people. There were also children. So it was still-- we were not in panic. We were just-- we were told that the parents are taken and the old people were taken care of. And the workers are going in a different place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived in front of the block, it was a wooden barrack for a thousand woman. And we were thirsty and terrible tired. It was a hot day. And they, they gave-- they let us drink from a barrel. And it was a bitter tasting liquid. What I find out later, that had iodine in it to calm the nerves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we drank not much because they didn't allow us to stay there too long and was herded in to the-- into the-- it was one, big room with the three tiers-- the-- of wooden planks for the beds. And nothing under. Just-- we got one blanket to cover us. 12 to a they called a [? coya ?], six and six. And we fall apart, we fell asleep, very early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me stop you for a moment there. Who was guarding you through this whole process?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- we were taken over by inmates, Czechoslovakian and Polish Jewish girls, who were there for a long time already. And they were not the kindest. They were under pressure. They had to be tough, because if they were not tough to us, then they suffered. The Nazis were making them to do the dirty work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- they were hardened already by that time. They had not much feeling for the newcomers. They were-- matter of fact, they were very angry, that you are stupid idiots, why did you come? Didn't you hear what happened to us? You had to come and do the same thing? It wasn't that enough who was burned already? And you will end up like everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were too tired. It didn't go into the mind really. We just couldn't accept. That was just too horrible. But unfortunately, a few days later, within a few days, we find out that they were telling the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So early in the morning we were called, it was waking. It was still dark outside. And we have to stand, shiver in the cold morning. They had guns. That was counting again and again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an hour later, Dr. Mengele came, and just pulled out from the rows whomever he didn't like the face and sent them straight to the crematorium. But the head counting went on. At that time, we had no number and no name, nothing. So that went on for three weeks. We were under observation. And whoever were sick people, a lot of diarrhea. The food was very little and very bad. The first two days, I couldn't eat it. And then when I was really, very hungry-- It was one dish for the six of us, of five of-- five of us. And one round dish, no spoon. And everybody was swallowed, and then give it to the other one. Swallowed, and give it to the next one, until we've finished up the dish. It was floating green weeds in it, with the clumps of earth still attached to it. It was horrible. But when you're very, really hungry, then you eat everything. Early in the morning, have to go to fetch the food from the kitchen. And they were also [? battle ?] because they were people there who-- were there-- Russians mainly, mainly Russian women-- they were stronger than we were. And they-- as we were carried that food every day, others were assigned to bring the big container of food. And so they wanted to grab some. One day, one day, almost break might back because I was-- I resistant-- I am-- was resisting to let them have it. And she got so angry, they gave her a dish into my spine, almost break it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So food was very scarce, very little, and very bad. Now, to go to the toilet facilities, we could go twice a day with, with a-- somebody was-- with a guard who was taking us to the washroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the water was not permissible to drink, because it was poison in it. It was all red. And they said, if you drink it, you will die. Some people didn't care and drunk it, and they got the diarrhea. It was horrible. At nighttime, we couldn't get out from the-- from the block. And they had a wheelbarrow as a toilet in front of the building, if somebody really had to go. But otherwise, it was strictly forbidden to, to leave the, the, the barrack where we stayed. And a thousand women together, miserable, and fighting, and arguing, and smelly, and there was no ventilation, and hungry, and everybody was just plain miserable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then finally, at three-- after the three weeks was over, I-- first, they-- they picked me to go to a place where it was a big dairy farming in [? Budin ?] [? Budin ?] or [? Budee ?]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Budin. ?] [? Budin. ?] It was close by the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, had you already been processed for the camp? Had you been tattooed already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You haven't been tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet. But I was-- they just took the blood from my ear to testing for TB. And I had to wait a few days. And that was when my friends who spoke-- these four girls, they spoke Czech and Slovak, and they find a lot of friends of theirs from the former-- who were brought over there much earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: So right away, they felt at home, and they had a little bit of privileges because her friends had the positions already. They were the first one who built up this place. So-- Who built up that particular place where you were housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the whole-- the whole-- the railway station was built by the Slovak girls and the first Hungarian groups of girls. The-- the sidings was leading to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: These particular sidings? These particular sidings that you're talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, was leading to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they built the-- the barracks. It was very primitive. Very-- if the wind was blowing through, the rain came in. So it was very primitive. And it was-- it was just a chaos, noisy, terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when-- when they picked me for this, to send me to [? Butin ?], my friends, somehow they were not picked. I-- and they knew already what [? Butin ?] means for-- stands for. They were very unhappy about me, but I didn't know. I couldn't do a thing. So I was waiting for my turn, that they, they will approve me going there. And luckily, the last minute, the Nazis decided there were too many Hungarian men were working there in the dairy farm, and they didn't want to bring Hungarian girls. Because we were not supposed to talk to each other. So I was left out from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, my friends were selected to-- they called [NON-ENGLISH]. That was a depot right by the train station where they were sorting out the clothes, what came with these unfortunate people, and also what they brought out from the crematorium. And they sent me occasionally a pair of shoes, a little bit of food, whatever they could. And then one day-- in the meantime, I forgot to tell that from day one, we-- we were not working under these three weeks while we were under observation, but they-- this [NON-ENGLISH], these leaders, they had a lot of work to do. They cleaned the inside, the barrack, and then they make a building, new, new projects, and they had to mix the cement.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they called out, who wants to go to work? And I jumped. I'm going. My friends told me that you are crazy. Why are you going? You don't have to. This is volunteer work. We will go when we have to.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. My father told me I have to work. And that saved me, because then a few weeks later, when they were selecting a group of women to work on Canada-- that was the elite, the best commando-- one of these Slovak girls whom I worked for her, pulled me out by hand and said, you pick her, she is a good worker. That's how I got into Canada. I would work there for six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before we get into that, tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you've already been processed into the camp now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when you-- tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I forgot to tell you that. Between-- after when [? Butin ?] fell through, they started to select people for-- for-- for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Registration?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Registration. And some of the women didn't want. They were hiding. They said they don't want. They should be stamped. This is degrading. I said, let me have it, as long as-- at least they know I am alive. And so when they put my number, and after that, I was called only by my number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about-- tell me about the tattooing process.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a long table outside, and there were a couple of girls. They were all inmates who did that. And they were just pricking, you know? Putting in the number. And they-- they wanted to know exactly-- the first time I was registered-- where I come from, the name of the street, the number of the street. That didn't mean anything. An hour later, they could send me to the crematorium, but they were very orderly. They made-- made-- it's possible that my name is still on whatever left, saved, of the papers, what they didn't destroy, the Nazis. It's possible that it's still there. But it didn't mean much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your number, Mrs. Rubinstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to look at it. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Isn't that funny?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a-- I have a mental block. A-6-6-6876. 78.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind just holding your arm up so that we can get a shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: 6878 78 or 76? It's a funny-- I never--

speaker: interviewee
text: 76. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Six-- yeah. Six-- I have a mental block. Funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many times you've probably heard that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you were then put through this process of being tattooed, what was the rest of your registration involving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and then I was selected to-- to this group of 350 Hungarian girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point-- up until this point, had you been subject to any mistreatment by guards or Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not-- not from the Nazis. From the inmates, yes. They were very rough, very. And the SS, we were scared of them. The women were the most vicious one, with the big dogs.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS women?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS women. But we-- we were standing in a line and just, you know, they counting heads and counting again. And then we were dismissed. I really-- the only one who really scared was Mengele. When he marched, marched on the main street, we knew that the [INAUDIBLE] of angels is coming because he came to pick. Then was a women with a daughter with us. And one day, they took the mother and the girl wanted to kill herself, but she couldn't do much. It's just-- there's a built-in self preservation that nobody wanted to-- volunteered to take me to. It was very painful. We got very close with the immediate who were around me. And then we, we knew. We felt everybody's pain and each other's pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at that point, after being processed, did you know of the gas chambers at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, definitely. And we were there. We saw the chimney was burning. We-- and when a big transport came in, then it-- they had-- they closed up. Nobody was allowed to go out from there. And then we knew that a transport arrived. They didn't want we should see it, and didn't want we should be panic. So while-- and after that, the chimney was burning day and night. So we knew that another transport of unfortunate people were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went to work at Canada. You were beginning to tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, that was a place where everything was a mountain. Mountain of eye glasses, a mountain of, of everything. And we had to open up the suitcases and the bags, whatever people brought with them. And most of them, their best thing they tried to bring, not knowing, not even-- that they are not going to be able to use it. So we had to sort it out and made it into big bales and everything went to Germany. Everything that was good. Shoes, and clothes, and beddings. Food was very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls worked in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 500.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there men that worked in there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not there. No. That was strictly women. The men came after to pick up the bales, what we packed together. And they carried it to the station to ship it out. And that was painful because we were close to the crematorium. But-- and for example, one day I opened up a suitcase and find a little dress in it, a red dress with little, white polka dots. It belonged to a little girl who was-- practically, I adopted her in the ghetto. The-- her mother, they were three small babies. She was the oldest. She was three years old, little Susie. And the mother was so busy with the babies and was miserable. The father was in a labor camp. And so that little Susie sat in my lap all the time, and I loved it. It was a cute little thing with a curly head of hair. And I knew what happened to little Susie with her mother and the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I here-- I open up a suitcase and find little Susie's dress. What she-- it was her favorite dress. And that dress meant so much, you know? It was-- it just opened up my whole tragedy. It was just very painful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And pictures I find from friends. You know, everybody tried to bring their photo album, hoping that they will save it. And it was very painful. Very. Every piece of clothes we knew that belonged to someone who is not here anymore. And then from there, we had a chance--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one moment. Were there any advantages to working in Canada for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's what I want to tell you. We could do a little bit of humanitarian work. Since we, we opened up the suitcases, we find medicine, and sometimes a little bit of food, and clothes, sweaters. And we tried to smuggle in whatever we could to the camp. But was not always possible because were inspection all the time, where they stripped us naked by the gate before we entered the camp. And it was very difficult. But occasionally, it happened. For example, shoes. Every morning I went to work with the torn, old shoes, and I walked back to the camp with the brand new shoes. And I left the old one behind. So in this case, I could help my friends who had shoes falling apart, and I could get a new one for them. And one day, a woman came to the gate before we just counted, over process to go through the main gate. A young woman crying that she gave birth to a baby in the middle of the night. She was in the hospital, they called it a [NON-ENGLISH]. And she knew that Mengele would come and inspect everyone. He will find her with the baby, that not just the baby will be killed, but she with the baby. We should bring her an aspirin, that she should kill her baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I looked at her. I said, isn't that enough killing going on? You want me-- I should be accomplice to killing a-- your baby? And she started to cry. She said, I am young. I hope my, my husband will make it. I hope I will survive. I can still have another child.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Mengele sends me to the crematorium, my whole family is gone. And this will be on your conscience. Please help me. I brought her the aspirin. I never saw her again. I didn't want to see her. But for years, I felt such a-- I had such a guilty feeling about it. And then we, we smuggled in gold watches to save someone's life. Because it was a lot of-- from the lice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you just for one moment? That woman that asked you to bring her an aspirin, do you know what became of her or of her child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if she killed her baby and give the aspirin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that if she made it, probably she disappeared from the-- left that, that child behind. And she went back to work. Mengele wasn't interested.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no more contact with her after that episode?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to know. I didn't want to know. We carried out letters because some of people worked in Canada. Later on, while I was working around the crematorium, we were the liaison bringing back and forth messages into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that you-- did you stop working at Canada while you were at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and the Hungarian transport started to slacken, you know, stop practically. End of June stopped because they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '44. I was there-- no, it was later on because I was three weeks-- in the middle of June, I was picked to go to Canada. And I was there for six weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the beginning of August, when no more transports came, the war was too close to Hungary. And they couldn't bring [INAUDIBLE]. It was the only [INAUDIBLE] they put up [INAUDIBLE] were left. And they had no time to bring them. So they stopped. The work was nothing to do there anymore. So I was back in the, in the, in the block. And then the following day, they were recruiting workers for the-- I didn't know for what, but I volunteered. And they took us out to build roads. The called it 103 commando.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were building roads from demolished houses bombed down by the Nazis. We had to take apart-- by hand, without any tool-- brick by brick the stones and carried to another crew who've were making the road. And then the other crew built convalescent home for the SS soldiers who returned from the war. They were still recuperating. So I worked on that one also for about six weeks or maybe two months. There, I lost my job again. And then came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just stop you there. What was that work like? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Carrying the stone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I--

speaker: interviewee
text: In a piece of, of leafs. Because there was nothing. We had no tools. On a leaf, we had to put the stones and carry. In and out, day in, day out. Carry these stones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who was guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they brutal towards you? Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally. I-- I was lucky. They didn't touch me. But many, unfortunately, especially if somebody was very pretty. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How far out of the camp would you go with that commando?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too far. That was the outskirt. That was still in Birkenau. That was still in, in, inside Birkenau. It was-- it was a big compound, but, but the working area was all-- all was around the crematorium and the railway station. Everything. The, the headquarters of the SS Nazis, the offices, Mengele's office, where he did all the-- with the twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Experiments. The [GERMAN], the sewing room. There were different sections was all-- was taken-- done by inmates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you suffering any physical difficulties at this point? Illness or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luckily, luckily I was strong. Luckily, I was well fed, and I was never sick before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. So I was lucky. Nothing, nothing serious really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just hungry. But so was everyone else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to organize food outside of your rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. It was no way. Food was impossible. Occasionally, we were working-- there was an old apple tree and-- with rotten apples in it because nobody tended it. And so we gobbled it up, with the worm and all. But we couldn't, we couldn't get any food. Food was impossible. While I was working in Canada we got little bundles where, you know, people were hoping to save some salamis, dried salamis. And then what we find a lot was diamonds and jewelry. But it didn't have any meaning. It is absolutely-- we couldn't use it. With a toothpaste stuck in-- wrapped over with thread. And we throw it to the toilet because we were scared of it. We couldn't buy a slice of bread with it. It had absolutely no meaning. Later on, it saved my life, the gold watch, but not in that-- in that time, it was, it didn't mean anything, jewelry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So carry on about your next job, after the road building job. Then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me-- I should tell you that while we were going out to work, we went in one way and the men and transport came in another way. They went in a different direction. We always had a chance, just-- just one word, one word, to call over. Did you hear? Do you know? Maybe you heard of my father?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day a man answered. Yes, I knew your father. Who are you? And the following day, I called back that my father's name was Elias. The following day, he called me because that was it, just we passed by. And the SS was marching there, we couldn't talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the following day he said, yes. I went to school with your father. And the following day, an onion was flying over with-- and he called out this is for Judith [? Schwartz ?] from me. And that night, we had a fantastic fiesta in our-- our-- I sliced up the onion, and everybody got a piece. And we had a wonderful-- a wonderful feeling that we had something extra. I met cousins of mine here and then an old friend there. And what was a very painful that-- in the beginning, end of July, about 5,000 Russian prisoner of war, young soldiers, were brought in to Birkenau. They let them wear their uniform. They didn't work. But every morning, they took them out for a walk. And they were marching, singing Russian songs, but I didn't understand. But they were so lively. They were so young. They were still not under nourished like we were. And so brave and singing beautiful patriotic songs. And we loved to-- loved to look at them. And then one day, there was no more song and no more soldier. So soon enough, we find out through the grapevine that one night, all 5,000 of them got sent into the crematorium. Nobody ever heard of them again. I wonder if the Nazis made a note or if they had anything left behind. But I think about it so often that these 5000 mothers were crying somewhere, never know that-- what happened to their sons. I still-- I hear their singing in my ear. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Unreal. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, I should be fair to tell-- to tell you that it was at least 85% or 90% were Jewish inmates, but there were others. I had a very good friend who was a German woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to stop here. We're--
